title: Bad ‘Game Of Thrones,’ ‘Stranger Things’ Endings Are Being Remade Convincingly, Poorly, With AI
text:
Innovation
Gaming
Bad ‘Game Of Thrones,’ ‘Stranger Things’ Endings Are Being Remade Convincingly, Poorly, With AI
By
Paul Tassi
,
Senior Contributor.
Forbes contributors publish independent expert analyses and insights.
News and opinion about video games, television, movies and the internet.
Follow Author

Game of Thrones
HBO
When AI video started first emerging a few years back, there was a claim that when it got sufficiently advanced, fans of a show would be able to just start generating their own episodes of the series based on what they wanted to see happen. Now, that’s begun to a certain extent with the arrival of a new AI model, as we see every other week at this point. But you can already see the problems here.
The new AI tech is
Seedance 2.0
, created by ByteDance, a Chinese technology company. It has instantly gone viral for its ability to render real-life actors convincingly, and a fight scene between Tom Cruise and Brad Pitt was shared all over the internet last week.
Now, however, we’re moving into different territory. Seedance is being used to “remake” what are viewed as poor endings to series like Game of Thrones and, more recently, Stranger Things, using the technology. Seeing these attempts, there is no doubt that:
A) It cannot be denied that this technology is rocketing forward at blinding speed, and these renditions of actors are the best they’ve ever been to date.
B) This is a wildly obvious violation of copyright, and while some IP holders let AI infringement lapse, Disney
has already gone hard
after ByteDance to get videos taken down.
MORE FOR YOU
C) The idea that fans can make “better” versions of shows reveals how hard that actually is to do when your only real talent ("talent") is entering prompts. The tech can do heavy lifting for rendering these characters, but its users cannot make something necessarily
good
out of that.
Here’s the “happy Game of Thrones” ending:
And a remake of the final battle of Stranger Things:
Some have noted that the special effects in these look more convincing than in the actual show, specifically the Stranger Things one. While that is very much debatable, if that is true in parts, this can only happen because it ignores things like budget, how something like this would actually need to be filmed within the constraints and production, and that it often ignores physics it claims it can replicate.
But I mean, these
are
these actors, at least 97% of the way there, raising the most serious concerns yet about using imagery of real-life actors or people to do or say whatever you want with a model. If you can make Jon Snow and Daenerys Targaryen get married, what could you make Kit Harington and Emilia Clarke do, outside of this? It’s a bit chilling.
I think it is somewhat disingenuous to say there is nothing noteworthy about this tech. What it’s capable of is insane, to some degree. But the idea of making your own versions of shows when you know nothing about filmmaking or writing or any of it means you are likely not producing any of value (this is true of most GenAI, many would argue). Still, this technology has the potential to be both disruptive and scary, and I think that’s why you’ve seen both fans push back so hard and corporations call their lawyers so quickly.
